title: Prehistory
end_expr: 4th millennium BCE (beginning of the ancient period)
description: The record of humankind from the emergence of the hominin lineage in Africa through the Paleolithic and Neolithic, ending with the rise of the first civilizations.

title: Ancient period
description: Era of early civilizations, regional empires, and classical antiquity, running from the cradles of civilization to the post-classical period.

title: Post-classical period
description: Period marked by the rise and spread of major religions, expanding civilization and trade, the Islamic Golden Age, and the Mongol invasions; some periodizations end it at 1450.

title: Early modern period
description: Era of European exploration and colonization, the Renaissance, Reformation, Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment, ending with the Industrial Revolution.

title: Modern period
description: Era of accelerating population growth, industrialization, world wars, and technological change, beginning around 1800 and continuing to the present.

title: Pre-Imperial China
start_expr: c. 2070 BCE (traditional start of Xia Dynasty)
description: The long era before China's political unification, encompassing the legendary dynasties, early Bronze Age states, and the fractured but culturally formative Zhou period.

title: Imperial China
description: More than two millennia of continuous imperial rule under successive dynasties, characterised by a Confucian bureaucratic state, the examination system, and expanding territorial and cultural influence.

title: Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors
description: Legendary prehistoric rulers credited in Chinese mythology with teaching humanity agriculture, writing, medicine, and statecraft; considered the cultural founders of Chinese civilization.

title: Xia Dynasty
description: The first dynasty in traditional Chinese historiography, semi-legendary but associated with early Bronze Age sites in the Yellow River valley; overthrown by the Shang.

title: Shang Dynasty
description: The first historically attested Chinese dynasty, known for oracle bone writing, advanced bronze casting, and walled cities centred on the Yellow River plain.

title: Western Zhou
description: The formative phase of Zhou rule, during which feudal lords owed allegiance to the Zhou king; Confucian tradition later idealised this era as a golden age of ritual and virtue.

title: Spring and Autumn Period
description: An era of weakening royal authority and rising regional powers named after the chronicle of the state of Lu; Confucius, Laozi, and Sunzi lived during this period.

title: Warring States Period
description: A period of intensifying warfare among seven major states that developed mass conscript armies, iron technology, and radical political philosophies including Legalism; ended by Qin's decisive conquest.

title: Qin Dynasty
description: China's first unified empire, which standardised weights, measures, currency, and script, began the Great Wall, and built the Terracotta Army — but collapsed within fifteen years of its founder's death.

title: Western Han
description: A long era of expansion and consolidation under the Han emperors, marked by the Silk Road trade, Confucian state orthodoxy, and military campaigns against the Xiongnu nomads.

title: Xin Dynasty
description: A brief interregnum established by Wang Mang, whose sweeping land and currency reforms provoked rebellions that restored the Han dynasty.

title: Eastern Han
end_expr: 220 CE (Cao Pi deposes last Han emperor)
description: The restored Han dynasty, with its capital moved east to Luoyang; notable for paper invention, seismograph, and Buddhism's arrival, but increasingly weakened by court factionalism and warlordism.

title: Three Kingdoms
description: Three rival states — Wei, Shu Han, and Wu — fought for supremacy after the Han collapse; immortalised in the Romance of the Three Kingdoms, one of China's most celebrated literary works.

title: Jin Dynasty
description: A brief reunification under the Sima clan followed by the Western Jin's collapse to nomadic invasions, forcing the court to flee south and establish the Eastern Jin in Jiankang.

title: Sixteen Kingdoms
description: A turbulent era of competing non-Chinese states in northern China following the Western Jin collapse, involving Xiongnu, Xianbei, Di, Jie, and Qiang rulers.

title: Northern and Southern Dynasties
description: China remained divided between a succession of southern dynasties at Jiankang and northern dynasties (chiefly Northern Wei and its successors) until Sui reunification; Buddhism flourished in both halves.

title: Sui Dynasty
description: A short-lived dynasty that reunified China after centuries of division, built the Grand Canal, and reformed the bureaucracy, but was exhausted by massive construction projects and failed Korean campaigns.

title: Tang Dynasty
end_expr: 907 CE (Zhu Wen deposes last Tang emperor)
description: Often considered China's golden age: cosmopolitan, internationally connected via the Silk Road, and culturally prolific in poetry, painting, and Buddhism; the reign of Empress Wu Zetian is uniquely notable.

title: Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms
description: A period of division following the Tang collapse, with five successive short-lived dynasties in the north and ten regional states in the south; ended when the Song dynasty reunified most of China.

title: Liao Dynasty
description: A Khitan empire that controlled Manchuria and northern China and exerted sustained political and cultural pressure on the Song dynasty; contemporaneous with the Five Dynasties and early Song.

title: Song Dynasty
end_expr: 1279 CE (last Song resistance crushed by Yuan)
description: An era of remarkable economic, technological, and cultural development — gunpowder weapons, movable type, paper money, and neo-Confucianism all flourished — though the dynasty was pushed south by Jurchen Jin in 1127.

title: Jin Dynasty (Jurchen)
description: A Jurchen-ruled empire that conquered the Liao and drove the Song south of the Huai River; eventually annihilated by a Mongol–Song alliance.

title: Yuan Dynasty
description: The Mongol-ruled dynasty established by Kublai Khan, connecting China to the vast Mongol Empire and enabling unprecedented Eurasian exchange; visited by Marco Polo and Ibn Battuta.

title: Ming Dynasty
end_expr: 1644 CE (Li Zicheng takes Beijing; Qing follows)
description: A native Chinese dynasty that rebuilt the Great Wall, completed the Forbidden City, and launched Zheng He's oceanic expeditions; later became increasingly isolationist and was overthrown by peasant revolt and Manchu invasion.

title: Qing Dynasty
description: The last imperial dynasty, founded by the Manchu Qing and expanding China to its greatest territorial extent; declined under Western pressure and internal rebellions, ending with the revolution of 1911.

title: Republic of China
end_expr: 1949 CE (PRC founded on mainland; ROC continues on Taiwan)
description: The republican era marked by warlordism, Japanese invasion, and civil war between Nationalists and Communists, ending when the Communist Party established the People's Republic on the mainland.

title: People's Republic of China
description: The Communist-ruled state founded after the civil war, which underwent the Great Leap Forward, Cultural Revolution, and subsequent market reforms that transformed China into a major global economic power.

title: Predynastic Egypt
description: The prehistoric period during which farming communities developed along the Nile, producing early pottery, trade networks, and the proto-writing and symbolic vocabulary that foreshadowed dynastic civilization.

title: Early Dynastic Period
description: The founding phase of pharaonic Egypt following Narmer's unification; the state apparatus, hieroglyphic writing, and the concept of divine kingship were consolidated under the first two dynasties.

title: Old Kingdom
description: The pyramid-building age, during which the great complexes at Giza and Saqqara were constructed; centralised royal power and a highly organised state administration reached their first peak.

title: First Intermediate Period
description: A period of fragmentation when central pharaonic authority collapsed and regional governors competed for power; traditionally associated with famine and instability, though regional arts also flourished.

title: Middle Kingdom
description: A period of reunification, expansion into Nubia, and cultural flourishing; classical Egyptian literature dates to this era, and the pharaohs pursued aggressive trade and military campaigns in the Levant.

title: Second Intermediate Period
description: The Hyksos from the Levant ruled Lower Egypt while native dynasties persisted in Upper Egypt and Nubia; the introduction of horse-drawn chariots, composite bows, and bronze weapons transformed Egyptian warfare.

title: New Kingdom
description: Egypt's imperial zenith: pharaohs including Thutmose III, Akhenaten, and Ramesses II extended rule into Nubia and the Levant, constructed Karnak and Abu Simbel, and fought the Hittites at Kadesh.

title: Third Intermediate Period
end_expr: 664 BCE (Psamtik I unifies under Saite Dynasty)
description: A period of political fragmentation with rival dynasties in the Delta and Libyan and Nubian rulers holding power at various times; Egypt's imperial reach contracted significantly.

title: Late Period
description: A final era of native and Persian pharaonic rule, marked by the Saite Renaissance of classical art, two Persian occupations, and Egyptian attempts to maintain independence against the Achaemenid Empire.

title: Achaemenid Egypt
description: Egypt as a satrapy of the Achaemenid Persian Empire, interrupted by a period of renewed native rule (404–343 BCE); Persian rulers were recognised as pharaohs but rule was often resented.

title: Ptolemaic Kingdom
description: A Macedonian Greek dynasty that ruled Egypt for nearly three centuries; Alexandria became the world's foremost centre of learning, and the Ptolemies adopted pharaonic titulature while ruling in Greek.

title: Roman Egypt
description: Egypt as a Roman province — grain basket of the empire — characterised by significant Greek and Jewish populations, the spread of Christianity, and continued use of hieroglyphs into the fourth century CE.

title: Byzantine Egypt
description: Egypt under Eastern Roman / Byzantine administration, dominated by Coptic Christianity, fierce theological disputes, and Sasanid Persian occupation (619–629 CE) before the Arab Islamic conquest.

title: Early Islamic Egypt
description: Egypt under successive caliphates — Rashidun, Umayyad, and Abbasid — during which Arabic replaced Greek and Coptic in administration and Islam gradually became the majority religion.

title: Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt
description: An Ismaili Shia caliphate that founded Cairo (al-Qahira) and built Al-Azhar mosque and university; Egypt became one of the wealthiest and most cultured states of the medieval world.

title: Ayyubid Sultanate
description: Founded by Saladin, the Ayyubid sultanate reunified Egypt and Syria, recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187, and became the foremost military power in the medieval Middle East.

title: Mamluk Sultanate
description: A sultanate ruled by military slaves who defeated the Mongols at Ain Jalut (1260) and expelled the last Crusader strongholds; Cairo was the cultural and commercial capital of the Arab world.

title: Ottoman Egypt
description: Egypt as an Ottoman province, nominally under a pasha but effectively governed by Mamluk beys; the country declined economically relative to its medieval peak but remained an important trade corridor.

title: Muhammad Ali Dynasty / Khedivate
description: An Albanian-Ottoman dynasty that modernised Egypt, built the Suez Canal (1869), and eventually came under British occupation (1882–1922); Egypt gained formal independence in 1922 but British influence persisted.

title: Republic of Egypt
description: A presidential republic founded after the 1952 revolution; Nasser nationalised the Suez Canal, Egypt led pan-Arab nationalism, fought multiple Arab-Israeli wars, and signed a peace treaty with Israel in 1979.

title: Norte Chico / Caral Civilization
description: One of the oldest known complex societies in the Americas, centered in coastal river valleys north of Lima; built monumental platform mounds and had sophisticated irrigation but no known writing or ceramics.

title: Chavín Culture
description: An influential cultural and religious horizon centred on the highland site of Chavín de Huántar; its distinctive jaguar-serpent iconography spread across much of the central Andes.

title: Paracas Culture
description: A coastal culture on the Paracas Peninsula known for extraordinary textile weaving, trepanation, and elaborate funerary bundles; preceded and influenced the Nazca culture.

title: Nazca Culture
description: A southern coastal culture famous for the Nazca Lines — vast geoglyphs etched into the desert — as well as polychrome pottery and extensive underground aqueducts (puquios).

title: Moche Culture
description: A northern coastal state-level society with monumental adobe pyramids, sophisticated irrigation, and highly realistic portrait ceramics; collapsed possibly due to prolonged El Niño drought events.

title: Tiwanaku
description: A highland city-state centered near Lake Titicaca that became a major Andean religious and political centre; its raised-field agriculture and iconography spread across the southern Andes.

title: Wari Empire
description: The first Andean empire, spreading from Ayacucho via a road network and administrative colonies; it introduced urban planning and state-level organisation to much of the Andes before its unexplained collapse.

title: Chimu Kingdom
description: The largest pre-Inca state on the Pacific coast, centred on the vast adobe city of Chan Chan; known for sophisticated goldsmithing and irrigation, it was conquered by the expanding Inca Empire.

title: Inca Empire
description: The largest empire in pre-Columbian America, stretching from Colombia to Chile and connected by thousands of kilometres of roads; the state managed resources through mit'a labour tribute rather than money.

title: Spanish Conquest of Peru
end_expr: 1572 CE (execution of Túpac Amaru I, last Inca ruler)
description: Francisco Pizarro's small force captured and executed Inca emperor Atahualpa in 1532; subsequent decades saw Spanish consolidation, destruction of Inca institutions, and the neo-Inca resistance at Vilcabamba.

title: Viceroyalty of Peru
description: Spain's largest South American colonial domain, centered on Lima; it administered most of South America for nearly three centuries and was the primary source of silver extracted at Potosí.

title: Peruvian War of Independence
end_expr: 1824 CE (Battle of Ayacucho ends Spanish rule)
description: Peru was among the last Spanish colonies to achieve independence; San Martín proclaimed independence in 1821, and Simón Bolívar's forces decisively defeated the Spanish royalists at Ayacucho in 1824.

title: Republic of Peru
description: The modern Peruvian state has experienced alternating periods of democratic and authoritarian rule, border conflicts, economic turbulence, and the Shining Path insurgency; economic growth since the 2000s has reduced poverty substantially.

title: Roman Kingdom
description: The legendary and early historical period of Rome's foundation under seven kings; while later Romans mythologised this era, archaeological evidence confirms a growing city and emerging Latin culture on the Tiber.

title: Roman Republic
description: The era of senatorial governance, citizen militias, and territorial expansion from a city-state to a Mediterranean empire; ended by a century of civil wars that ultimately produced the principate.

title: Early Roman Republic
description: The formative republican period during which Rome unified the Italian peninsula through a combination of conquest and alliance; the Struggle of the Orders gradually extended political rights to plebeians.

title: Middle Roman Republic
description: Rome fought and won the Punic Wars against Carthage, conquered Greece and the Hellenistic east, and emerged as the dominant Mediterranean power; vast new wealth strained the traditional social order.

title: Late Roman Republic
description: Decades of political crisis — the Gracchi, Marius, Sulla, Caesar, and the triumvirates — as the senatorial system failed to manage an empire, culminating in Julius Caesar's assassination and the final civil war.

title: Roman Empire
description: Five centuries of imperial rule over a vast multi-ethnic state spanning from Britain to Mesopotamia; it produced Roman law, the Latin language, widespread urbanisation, and ultimately the spread of Christianity.

title: Principate
description: The first phase of the empire, in which emperors maintained the republican façade while holding ultimate power; the Julio-Claudian, Flavian, and Antonine dynasties are the most celebrated.

title: Pax Romana
description: Roughly two centuries of relative peace, prosperity, and stability across most of the empire under Augustus and the five Good Emperors; the Roman road network, aqueducts, and legal system flourished.

title: Crisis of the Third Century
description: Near-collapse of the empire: fifty years of military anarchy, barbarian invasions, plague, and economic disruption saw over twenty emperors claim power, many assassinated within months of accession.

title: Dominate
description: Diocletian and Constantine reorganised the empire with an overtly autocratic style, the Tetrarchy, a new capital at Constantinople, and Christianisation; the Western half progressively fragmented under barbarian pressure.

title: Western Roman Empire
start_expr: 395 CE (death of Theodosius I; formal division)
description: The last decades of Roman rule in the west, plagued by invasions of Visigoths, Vandals, Huns, and other peoples; the final emperor was a teenager deposed without ceremony by a barbarian general.

title: Eastern Roman Empire
description: The continuation of the Roman Empire in the east, known to historians as the Byzantine Empire; it preserved Roman law and Greek culture, repelled many invasions, and finally fell to the Ottoman Turks after a millennium of survival.

title: Ubaid Period
description: A prehistoric cultural horizon spread across Mesopotamia characterised by painted pottery, the first known temples on mud-brick platforms, and early agricultural villages that prefigured the Sumerian city-states.

title: Uruk Period
description: The world's first urban civilisation emerged at Uruk (Warka), which grew to perhaps 50,000 inhabitants; this period produced the earliest writing (proto-cuneiform), cylinder seals, and monumental temples.

title: Jemdet Nasr Period
description: A transitional phase between the Uruk and Early Dynastic periods, during which cuneiform writing expanded and multi-city administrative networks developed across southern Mesopotamia.

title: Early Dynastic Period
description: Sumerian city-states — Ur, Lagash, Uruk, Nippur — competed for hegemony; the Royal Tombs of Ur date to this era, as does the Epic of Gilgamesh's earliest strands and the emergence of professional armies.

title: Akkadian Empire
description: The world's first recorded empire, created by Sargon of Akkad, who conquered all Mesopotamia and parts of Syria and Anatolia; his daughter Enheduanna is the world's first named author.

title: Gutian Interlude
description: A poorly documented period of Gutian dominance from the Zagros Mountains following the Akkadian collapse; Sumerian cities recovered gradually and power shifted between regional centres.

title: Third Dynasty of Ur
description: A Neo-Sumerian renaissance under Ur-Nammu and Shulgi that produced the world's oldest known law code, an elaborate bureaucratic state, and literary compositions that defined the Sumerian canon.

title: Old Babylonian Period
description: Hammurabi of Babylon (r. 1792–1750 BCE) unified Mesopotamia and issued the famous law code; the period also saw the composition of Gilgamesh in near-final form and sophisticated mathematics.

title: Kassite Babylonia
description: A Kassite dynasty from the Zagros ruled Babylon for over four centuries — the longest-lived Babylonian dynasty — maintaining Sumero-Akkadian traditions while developing extensive international diplomacy with Egypt and the Hittites.

title: Middle Assyrian Period
description: Assyria emerged as a major power rivalling the Hittites and Mitanni; Middle Assyrian law codes survive from this era, and the state developed the administrative and military traditions the Neo-Assyrian Empire would perfect.

title: Neo-Assyrian Empire
end_expr: 609 BCE (fall of Harran; Assyrian state ends)
description: The most powerful state of the ancient Near East, stretching from Egypt to Iran; Assyrian kings like Tiglath-Pileser III, Sargon II, and Ashurbanipal created the first true empire with systematic deportation, garrison cities, and a royal library at Nineveh.

title: Neo-Babylonian Empire
description: Nebuchadnezzar II destroyed Jerusalem, deported the Jews to Babylon, and rebuilt the city with the Hanging Gardens and Ishtar Gate; the empire lasted less than a century before falling to Persia without a siege.

title: Achaemenid Mesopotamia
description: Babylon became a wealthy satrapy and sometime capital of the Achaemenid Persian Empire; Cyrus restored exiles and respected local cults, and the city remained a major commercial and cultural centre.

title: Seleucid Mesopotamia
description: The Seleucid successor state to Alexander's empire controlled Mesopotamia for over 150 years; Seleucia-on-the-Tigris was founded as a new administrative centre and cuneiform scholarship continued into this era.

title: Parthian Mesopotamia
end_expr: 224 CE (Ardashir I defeats last Parthian king)
description: The Arsacid Parthian Empire ruled Mesopotamia for three and a half centuries, repelling Roman invasions at Carrhae (53 BCE) and in the second century CE; Ctesiphon on the Tigris became the imperial capital.

title: Sassanid Mesopotamia
end_expr: 651 CE (last Sassanid emperor killed; Arab conquest complete)
description: The Sassanid Persian Empire made Ctesiphon its capital and competed with Rome and Byzantium for centuries; Mesopotamia was the empire's heartland, an agrarian and commercial centre with a cosmopolitan population.

title: Early Islamic Caliphates
start_expr: 637 CE (Arab forces defeat Sassanids at al-Qadisiyyah)
description: The Rashidun and Umayyad caliphates rapidly conquered Mesopotamia; Arabic replaced Aramaic in administration, and the new garrison cities of Basra and Kufa became centres of early Islamic scholarship.

title: Abbasid Caliphate
description: The Abbasid caliphs built Baghdad in 762 CE, which grew into the world's largest city and the centre of the Islamic Golden Age; mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and philosophy flourished until the catastrophic Mongol invasion.

title: Mongol Iraq / Ilkhanate
description: The Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258 killed the last Abbasid caliph and devastated the agricultural infrastructure of Iraq; the Ilkhanate eventually converted to Islam and assimilated, before collapsing into competing successor states.

title: Ottoman Iraq
description: The Ottoman Empire ruled the three vilayets of Mosul, Baghdad, and Basra for nearly four centuries; the region was a frontier zone contested with Safavid Persia and remained economically underdeveloped relative to its ancient past.

title: Nok Culture
description: An iron-smelting culture in central Nigeria famous for its terracotta figurines — the earliest known large-scale sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa — and for introducing iron technology to the region.

title: Djenné-Djenno
description: One of the earliest known cities in sub-Saharan Africa, located on an island in the Niger River delta; it predates the Arab-influenced cities of the Sahel and shows indigenous urban development with extensive long-distance trade.

title: Trans-Saharan Trade Era
end_expr: c. 1600 CE (Atlantic trade supersedes overland routes)
description: The height of overland commerce crossing the Sahara, exchanging West African gold, salt, and enslaved people for North African and Mediterranean goods; the wealth it generated built the great Sahelian empires.

title: Ghana Empire
description: The first of the great Sahelian empires, controlling the gold and salt trade across the western Sahara from its capital Koumbi Saleh; its power attracted Arab geographers and eventually Islamic conquest pressure.

title: Kanem-Bornu Empire
description: A remarkably long-lived empire centred on Lake Chad that converted to Islam in the eleventh century and survived over a millennium of dynastic changes, migrations, and external pressures before colonisation.

title: Mali Empire
description: The most powerful medieval West African empire, which controlled the gold and salt trade and at its height extended from the Atlantic coast to Timbuktu; Mansa Musa's 1324 pilgrimage to Mecca famously distributed so much gold it depressed prices across the Mediterranean.

title: Songhai Empire
end_expr: 1591 CE (Moroccan army defeats Songhai at Tondibi)
description: The largest empire in West African history, controlling the middle Niger and the great cities of Timbuktu, Djenné, and Gao; its fall to a Moroccan musket-armed invasion opened the region to decades of instability.

title: Jolof Empire
description: A Wolof state on the Senegambian coast that unified several kingdoms under a paramount ruler; its fragmentation coincided with the arrival of Portuguese traders who offered coastal states direct access to Atlantic commerce.

title: Benin Kingdom
start_expr: c. 1180 CE (Eweka I founds Oba dynasty)
description: A powerful Edo kingdom in southern Nigeria renowned for its sophisticated bronze casting, coral-beaded court regalia, and extensive system of defensive earthworks; the kingdom's treasures were looted by British forces in 1897.

title: Oyo Empire
description: A Yoruba empire that became the dominant power in the western Sudan and coastal region, built on cavalry and long-distance trade; it played a significant role in the Atlantic slave trade before collapsing under Fulani pressure.

title: Atlantic Slave Trade
description: European merchants transported an estimated 12 million enslaved Africans across the Atlantic; West Africa was the primary source region, profoundly disrupting states, fuelling warfare, and reshaping the demographic and political landscape of the region.

title: Kingdom of Dahomey
description: A centralised Fon kingdom in present-day Benin known for its military prowess — including the all-female Agojie warriors — its palace art, and its prominent participation in and eventual resistance to the Atlantic slave trade.

title: Ashanti Empire
start_expr: 1701 CE (Osei Tutu founds confederacy; Golden Stool legend)
description: A powerful Akan state in present-day Ghana whose wealth derived from gold and kola trade; its military confederation successfully resisted multiple British invasions before being incorporated into the Gold Coast colony.

title: Sokoto Caliphate
description: A large Fulani Islamic state established through a series of reformist jihads across the central Sahel; at its height it was the largest state in Africa south of the Sahara and one of the most populous Islamic states in the world.

title: Colonial Period
end_expr: 1960 CE (Year of Africa; most colonies gain independence)
description: European powers — primarily Britain, France, and Portugal — partitioned West Africa following the 1884–85 Berlin Conference; colonial rule imposed new borders, extracted resources, and restructured economies, creating lasting political and social legacies.

title: Post-Independence Era
start_expr: 1957 CE (Ghana becomes first sub-Saharan colony to gain independence)
description: Beginning with Ghana's independence under Kwame Nkrumah, West African nations gained sovereignty through the 1960s; the era has been marked by nation-building, military coups, resource wealth, rapid population growth, and the ongoing development of regional institutions such as ECOWAS.
